    pub timestamp: u64,
    pub message: String,
}

title: 17  VCS Engine
text:
17.3 Merge Options
MergeOptions controls merge behavior:
pub struct MergeOptions {
    pub no_commit: bool,   // stage result without committing
    pub ff_only: bool,     // fail if not fast-forwardable
    pub no_ff: bool,       // force merge commit even on fast-forward
    pub squash: bool,      // collapse into a single non-merge commit
    pub message: Option<String>,  // custom commit message
}

title: 17  VCS Engine
text:
17.4 Cherry-Pick and GC Options
CherryPickOptions controls cherry-pick behavior: no_commit stages without committing, record_origin appends the source commit ID to the message.
GcOptions has a single dry_run field. When true, gc::gc reports unreachable objects without deleting them.

title: 17  VCS Engine
text:
17.5 DAG Algorithms
All DAG algorithms operate on the commit graph \(G = (V, E)\) where \(V\) is the set of commits and \(E\) is the parent relation.

Merge base: Two-frontier BFS. We alternately expand from each side; the first vertex visited by both frontiers is the lowest common ancestor. For commits \(a\) and \(b\), this finds \(\text{LCA}(a, b) = \arg\min_{c \in \text{Anc}(a) \cap \text{Anc}(b)} \text{depth}(c)\). Complexity: \(O(|V|)\) worst case, typically much less.
Path finding: BFS backwards from target, reconstruct via parent map. Returns the sequence of commits \((c_0, c_1, \ldots, c_k)\) where \(c_0\) is the source and \(c_k\) is the target.
Log walk: Max-heap ordered by timestamp for topological-chronological order. Each commit is visited exactly once (tracked via a HashSet). Merge commits push both parents onto the heap. Complexity: \(O(n \log n)\) for \(n\) commits.
Compose path: Given a path \((c_0, \ldots, c_k)\), chains migrations via panproto_mig::compose. The composed migration \(M_{0 \to k} = M_{k-1 \to k} \circ \cdots \circ M_{0 \to 1}\) transforms data from schema \(S_0\) to schema \(S_k\).
Is ancestor: BFS reachability check from candidate to target. Returns true if there exists a path in \(G\) from the candidate to the target following parent edges.







NoteWhy a max-heap for log walk instead of simple DFS?



DFS would visit one branch fully before starting the other, producing non-chronological output for interleaved branches. The timestamp-ordered heap interleaves branches chronologically, matching what users expect from git log.

title: 17  VCS Engine
text:
17.6 Repository Operations
Beyond the core commit flow, repo.rs exposes several additional operations:

Repository::amend(): creates a commit with the same parent(s) as the current HEAD but updated schema, migration, and message. The previous HEAD commit becomes unreachable (collectible by gc).
refs::force_delete_branch: deletes a branch regardless of merge status
refs::rename_branch: renames a branch ref, updating HEAD if the current branch is renamed
refs::create_and_checkout_branch: creates a branch at HEAD and switches to it atomically
refs::create_annotated_tag: creates a TagObject in the store and points a tag ref at it
refs::create_tag_force: overwrites an existing tag ref
stash::stash_apply: re-applies the top stash entry without removing it from the stack
stash::stash_show: returns the schema diff between the stash entry and its parent
stash::stash_clear: drops all stash entries and removes the refs/stash ref

title: 17  VCS Engine
text:
17.7 Error Variants
VcsError is #[non_exhaustive] and includes:

ObjectNotFound, RefNotFound, BranchNotFound, TagNotFound
BranchAlreadyExists, TagAlreadyExists
NotOnBranch, DetachedHead, IndexEmpty, IndexNotEmpty
MergeConflict, RebaseConflict, CherryPickConflict
FastForwardRequired, NothingToAmend
StashEmpty, InvalidRef, CorruptObject
RemoteNotSupported

title: 17  VCS Engine
text:
17.8 Three-Way Merge (Combining Diverged Branches)
Given schemas base, ours, theirs, the merge computes a combined result. Categorically, this is a pushout (colimit) over the shared base.1

diff(base, ours) and diff(base, theirs): the extended diff covers all 13 schema fields (vertices, edges, constraints, hyper-edges, required, NSIDs, variants, orderings, recursion points, usage modes, spans, nominal)
For every element in every field, classify its fate on each side: unchanged, added, removed, or modified
Apply the merge rule: one-sided changes accepted; identical changes deduplicated; incompatible changes flagged as typed conflicts with the base value retained
No tie-breaking: the merge is commutative, meaning merge(base, A, B) == merge(base, B, A)
Rebuild precomputed indices
Derive migrations from ours to merged and theirs to merged via auto_mig::derive_migration







TipWhy is commutativity important for merge?



Commutativity means the merge result doesn’t depend on which branch we call “ours” vs “theirs.” This eliminates an entire class of bugs where merge order affects the outcome. It’s a stronger guarantee than git’s text merge, which can produce different conflict markers depending on merge direction.

title: 17  VCS Engine
text:
17.9 Rename Detection
The rename_detect module provides heuristic rename detection between schema versions. When auto_mig::derive_migration encounters a vertex that is absent in one schema version and a structurally similar vertex appears in the other, the rename detector can identify this as a rename rather than a delete-and-add.

17.9.1 Algorithm
For vertex rename detection (detect_vertex_renames):

Collect removed vertices (in old, not in new) and added vertices (in new, not in old)
For each (removed, added) pair, compute structural similarity:

Same vertex kind: +0.3
Same outgoing edge names: +0.3
Same incoming edge names: +0.2
Edit distance of vertex ID names <= 3: +0.2

Greedy bipartite matching: take highest-scoring pairs first
Return pairs >= threshold

For edge label rename detection (detect_edge_renames), the algorithm compares removed and added edges between the same surviving vertex pair, scoring by name edit distance normalized to string length.


17.9.2 CommitObject Integration
Detected renames are stored in CommitObject.renames as Vec<SiteRename>. This field is #[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Vec::is_empty")], so old commits without renames deserialize correctly.
See Section 18.2 for the full naming and identity architecture.

title: 17  VCS Engine
text:
17.10 Related Work and Differentiation
The term “schematic version control” distinguishes panproto from “semantic version control” (AST-aware diffing tools for source code) and from schema registries (which store schema versions linearly without branching or merge).

17.10.1 Semantic/Structural Version Control for Source Code
These tools bring structure-awareness to source code via AST/CST parsing. They understand syntax but not the semantics of the data being versioned.

17.10.1.1 sem and weave (Ataraxy Labs)
sem is a Rust CLI that provides entity-level diff, blame, graph, and impact analysis for code across 16 languages via tree-sitter. It uses structural hashing to distinguish cosmetic changes from real logic changes. weave is its companion merge driver, which resolves conflicts at the entity level and achieves 31/31 clean merges on a benchmark where git manages 15/31.
Both tools sit on top of git. They don’t replace git’s object store or DAG; they provide a smarter view of git’s existing data. sem parses source code into entity trees (functions, classes, interfaces). panproto-vcs operates on a different kind of object: mathematical schema structure expressed as GAT models with sorts (types in a formal theory, not sorting operations), operations, and equations. weave’s merge works by entity-level pattern matching. panproto-vcs’s merge computes the combined result over a shared base theory (a pushout in the category of GATs).


17.10.1.2 Cow (Tikhon Jelvis)
Cow is a Haskell project that diffs and merges parse trees instead of text. It uses a modification of the Wagner-Fischer algorithm to compute tree edit distance, and can detect moves and group related changes.
Cow works on generic parse trees and is language-agnostic. panproto-vcs works on typed schema graphs where the types (sorts, operations, equations) constrain what counts as a valid change. Cow’s merge minimizes edit distance; panproto-vcs computes a merge with universal-property guarantees that edit-distance approaches lack.


17.10.1.3 difftastic (Wilfred Hughes)
difftastic is a Rust structural diff tool using tree-sitter for 30+ languages. It produces human-readable diffs that understand syntax and ignore whitespace, but it’s purely a diff tool. It can’t merge, has no VCS, and has no schema awareness.


17.10.1.4 Mergiraf
Mergiraf is a Rust syntax-aware three-way merge tool for git using tree-sitter, supporting 33 languages. When the AST merge fails, it falls back to line-based merging.
The fallback is the important difference. Mergiraf is heuristic: when its structured approach fails, it silently degrades to a less-informed algorithm. panproto-vcs’s merge always produces a result. Conflicts are reported explicitly, never papered over.


17.10.1.5 SemanticMerge, Spork, JDime, jFSTMerge, IntelliMerge
SemanticMerge (Plastic SCM / Unity) is a commercial structure-based merge for C#, Java, VB.NET, and C. Spork is a Java-only AST merge using the 3DM-Merge algorithm. JDime, jFSTMerge, and IntelliMerge are academic structured merge tools for Java.
All of these are language-specific merge tools for source code. panproto-vcs is protocol-parametric: the same merge algorithm works across 77 data formats because it operates on the abstract schema graph.



17.10.2 Theoretical Proposals

17.10.2.1 Structure-aware VCS via observational bridge types (Topos Institute 2024)
This proposal uses logical relations and bridge types from HoTT to formally specify what constitutes a valid structural change. It applies parametricity to version control. There is no implementation.
This is the closest work in mathematical spirit. Both panproto-vcs and the Topos proposal use type-theoretic foundations to define valid changes. panproto-vcs uses GATs (Cartmell 1986); the Topos proposal uses bridge types from cubical type theory. The two approaches may be compatible: bridge types could serve as an alternative formalization of migration validity within panproto’s framework.


17.10.2.2 A Categorical Theory of Patches (Mimram and Di Giusto 2013)
Mimram and Di Giusto define a category where objects are files and morphisms are patches, with merge defined as a pushout.2 They investigate the free completion of this category under finite merges and give an explicit description where objects are finite sets labeled by lines equipped with a transitive relation.
panproto-vcs implements this idea for schemas rather than text files. The objects in Mimram and Di Giusto’s category are sequences of lines, a flat structure. panproto’s objects are models of GATs: typed graphs with protocol-specific sorts, operations, equations, and constraints. The richer structure gives the merge more to work with.


17.10.2.3 DVCS for Structured Data (Schürmann 2020)
Schurmann’s manifesto calls for version control over relational algebra and algebraic datatypes. It identifies the gap but provides no implementation. panproto-vcs realizes this vision.



17.10.3 Algebraic/Categorical VCS

17.10.3.1 Darcs
Darcs is a Haskell VCS where patches are algebraic objects with inverse and commutation operations. Merge works by finding a reordering of patches that lets both sides apply cleanly.
Darcs’s patches are syntactic: line insertions and deletions that can commute when they touch different lines. panproto’s migrations are semantic: vertex and edge maps that compose associatively and merge over shared structure. Darcs has no notion of typed structure.3


17.10.3.2 Pijul
Pijul is a Rust DVCS based on Mimram and Di Giusto (2013)’s categorical patch theory. Merge is the pushout in a category of files and patches, giving commutativity and associativity.4
Pijul’s objects are byte-chunk graphs, essentially ordered sequences of text blocks. panproto’s are models of GATs with protocol-specific theories, constraints, and edge rules. panproto’s merge has access to type information that lets it distinguish “both branches renamed vertex X” (conflict) from “both branches added unrelated vertices” (clean merge). Pijul can’t make this distinction. Additionally, panproto can migrate instance data through the DAG via lifting, which requires understanding the semantics of the objects being versioned.



17.10.4 Schema Migration Tools
These tools manage schema evolution but aren’t version control systems. They lack content-addressing, commit DAGs, branching, and structural merge.

17.10.4.1 Cambria (Litt et al. 2021)
Cambria is a JS/TS library for JSON schema evolution via bidirectional lenses, using combinators (rename, add, remove, hoist, wrap). It targets local-first and CRDT-based collaboration.
panproto subsumes Cambria’s combinator model in panproto-lens. Cambria operates only on JSON; panproto works across 76 protocols. Cambria has no VCS layer. Cambria’s convert combinator breaks the lens laws, as acknowledged by Litt et al. (2021); panproto preserves round-trip fidelity via explicit complement tracking.


17.10.4.2 buf (Buf Technologies)
Buf is a Go tool for Protobuf schema linting, breaking change detection, and schema registry. It integrates with CI/CD to enforce compatibility.
Buf is a registry and linter for a single protocol. It relies on git for version control, can’t migrate instance data, has no merge algorithm, and doesn’t work across protocol boundaries.


17.10.4.3 Atlas (Ariga)
Atlas is a Go tool for declarative database schema management, supporting several SQL dialects. It generates migration SQL scripts from declarative schema definitions.
Atlas is database-specific and generates imperative migration scripts. It relies on git for version history. panproto-vcs is protocol-parametric and provides its own version control with structural merge.


17.10.4.4 Prisma Migrate, Flyway, Liquibase
These tools produce linear migration sequences (SQL scripts). No branching, no merge, no content-addressing.



17.10.5 Schema Registries
Schema registries store schema versions but don’t provide version control. Versions are linearly ordered per subject with no branching or merge.

17.10.5.1 Confluent Schema Registry
Confluent’s Schema Registry provides a REST API for Avro, JSON Schema, and Protobuf schemas, with compatibility checking modes (BACKWARD, FORWARD, FULL, etc.) and linear version history per subject. Schemas are identified by sequential version numbers rather than by their structure.


17.10.5.2 SchemaVer, AWS Glue Schema Registry, Apicurio
SchemaVer is a versioning scheme (MODEL-REVISION-ADDITION), analogous to SemVer for schemas. panproto-vcs uses content-addressing: identity is derived from structure, not assigned. AWS Glue Schema Registry and Apicurio are cloud and self-hosted schema registries for event streaming, similar to Confluent.

title: 17  VCS Engine
text:
The merge combines the two branch schemas along their shared base, producing a unified result. Formally, let \(S_b\) be the base schema, \(S_o\) the “ours” schema, and \(S_t\) the “theirs” schema. The merged schema \(S_m\) is the pushout of the span \(S_o \leftarrow S_b \rightarrow S_t\) in the category of schemas.↩︎
The merge combines structures along shared elements, producing a universal result. Categorically, this is a pushout (colimit).↩︎
Both Darcs and Pijul use algebraic patch theories, but their objects are untyped text (lines and insertions). panproto’s objects are typed schema graphs with sorts, operations, and equations, which allows the merge to exploit type information for conflict detection.↩︎
Both Pijul and panproto-vcs use pushouts for merge. The difference is in the objects being merged.↩︎

title: 18  Advanced Internals: Building Blocks, Naming, Auto-Morphisms, and Type Checking
text:
18.1 Building-Block Theories
panproto defines 27 building-block GATs. We’ll build up from the three simplest, then catalog the rest.
crumbs: 18  Advanced Internals: Building Blocks, Naming, Auto-Morphisms, and Type Checking

title: 18  Advanced Internals: Building Blocks, Naming, Auto-Morphisms, and Type Checking
text:
18.1.1 Formal Invariants
Every building-block theory satisfies:

Sort closure: every sort (a type in a formal theory, not a sorting operation) name in an operation’s input/output types is declared in the theory’s sorts list.
Equation well-typedness: both sides of every equation have the same output sort.
No tautologies: \(\text{lhs} \neq \text{rhs}\) syntactically for all equations.



18.1.2 The Property-to-Structure Principle
Several building blocks encode conditional properties as unconditional equations by adding operations and dependent sorts.1 For example, injectivity of coproduct injection is not injection(v1) = injection(v2) -> v1 = v2 (a conditional equation, not expressible in GATs). Instead, ThCoproduct adds a retraction variant_of : Vertex -> Variant with the equation variant_of(injection(v)) = v. The retraction is structure, not a property.
This separation yields a clean boundary: structural properties (transitivity, injectivity, uniqueness, optionality) belong in GAT sorts+equations; validation properties (cardinality, patterns, ranges) belong in ThConstraint.


18.1.3 The ThGraph to ThReflexiveGraph to ThCoproduct Progression
The simplest theory is ThGraph: just vertices, edges, and two operations.
ThGraph
  Sorts: Vertex, Edge
  Ops:   src : Edge → Vertex
         tgt : Edge → Vertex
  Eqs:   (none)
ThReflexiveGraph extends ThGraph with identity edges and two equations that force id to be a section of both src and tgt:
ThReflexiveGraph
  Sorts: Vertex, Edge
  Ops:   id  : Vertex → Edge
         src : Edge → Vertex
         tgt : Edge → Vertex
  Eqs:   src(id(v)) = v
         tgt(id(v)) = v
Type check: id(v) : Edge, src(id(v)) : Vertex, v : Vertex. Both sides have sort Vertex.
Without the equations, this is just ThGraph + an extra operation. The equations make the identity edge load-bearing.
ThCoproduct adds the retraction pattern to encode injectivity:
ThCoproduct
  Sorts: Vertex, Variant, Tag
  Ops:   injection : Variant → Vertex
         tag : Variant → Tag
         variant_of : Vertex → Variant
  Eq:    variant_of(injection(v)) = v
Type check: injection(v) : Vertex, variant_of(injection(v)) : Variant, v : Variant. Both sides have sort Variant.
The equation variant_of(injection(v)) = v encodes that variant_of is a left inverse of injection. This is how panproto represents “injection is injective” without conditional equations.






NoteWhy can’t we just write injection(v1) = injection(v2) implies v1 = v2?



GATs only support unconditional equations. Conditional equations (implications) would require dependent type theory or first-order logic. The retraction trick sidesteps this by adding structure (the variant_of operation) that witnesses the property.




18.1.4 Theories with Equations

18.1.4.1 ThRecursion: Fold-Unfold
Sorts: Vertex, Mu
Ops:   fold : Vertex → Mu
       unfold : Mu → Vertex
Eq:    unfold(fold(v)) = v
Type check: fold(v) : Mu, unfold(fold(v)) : Vertex, v : Vertex. Both sides: Vertex.
This is the isorecursive fold-unfold law. fold is a section of unfold.


18.1.4.2 ThReflexiveGraph: Identity Edges
Sorts: Vertex, Edge
Ops:   id : Vertex → Edge
       src : Edge → Vertex
       tgt : Edge → Vertex
Eqs:   src(id(v)) = v
       tgt(id(v)) = v
Type check: id(v) : Edge, src(id(v)) : Vertex, v : Vertex. Both sides: Vertex.


18.1.4.3 ThSymmetricGraph: Involution
Sorts: Vertex, Edge
Ops:   inv : Edge → Edge
       src : Edge → Vertex
       tgt : Edge → Vertex
Eqs:   src(inv(e)) = tgt(e)
       tgt(inv(e)) = src(e)
       inv(inv(e)) = e
Type checks:

src(inv(e)) : Vertex, tgt(e) : Vertex
inv(inv(e)) : Edge, e : Edge



18.1.4.4 ThCoproduct: Variant Retraction
Sorts: Vertex, Variant, Tag
Ops:   injection : Variant → Vertex
       tag : Variant → Tag
       variant_of : Vertex → Variant
Eq:    variant_of(injection(v)) = v
Type check: injection(v) : Vertex, variant_of(injection(v)) : Variant, v : Variant. Both sides: Variant.


18.1.4.5 ThPartial: Definedness Witness
Sorts: Vertex, Defined
Ops:   defined : Vertex → Defined
       witness : Defined → Vertex
Eq:    defined(witness(d)) = d
Type check: witness(d) : Vertex, defined(witness(d)) : Defined, d : Defined. Both sides: Defined.

title: 18  Advanced Internals: Building Blocks, Naming, Auto-Morphisms, and Type Checking
section: 18.2 Naming, Identity, and the Morphism Tower
text:
18.2 Naming, Identity, and the Morphism Tower
This section covers the panproto-gat identifier abstraction, the unified naming layer, the morphism tower that cascades theory-level renames into data migrations, and the VCS rename detection heuristic.

18.2.1 The Ident Abstraction
File: crates/panproto-gat/src/ident.rs
The Ident type separates stable identity from display name, following GATlab (Lynch et al. 2024):
pub struct Ident {
    pub scope: ScopeTag,   // which theory/schema this belongs to
    pub index: u32,        // positional index within scope
    pub name: Arc<str>,    // human-readable display name
}
PartialEq and Hash use only (scope, index), making comparisons \(O(1)\) regardless of name length. Renaming an identifier (via ident.renamed("new_name")) doesn’t change its identity, so HashMap<Ident, _> entries survive renames without rehashing.
ScopeTag is a monotonically increasing u32 assigned via ScopeTag::fresh(). Each Theory or SchemaBuilder gets a unique scope at construction time. ScopeTag::LEGACY (scope 0) is used for deserializing old-format data via Ident::from_legacy().


18.2.2 The Name Type
File: crates/panproto-gat/src/ident.rs
Name wraps Arc<str> with a pointer-equality fast path:
pub struct Name(pub Arc<str>);
Equality checks use Arc::ptr_eq first (single pointer comparison), falling back to string comparison only on pointer mismatch. In the restrict hot path, both sides typically originate from the same schema construction, so the fast path hits frequently.
Key trait implementations:

Deref<Target=str> and Borrow<str>: HashMap<Name, V>::get("string_literal") works
From<String>, From<&str>: zero-friction conversion from string types
Default: empty string
serde::Serialize/Deserialize: transparent (serializes as a plain string)

Performance impact on wtype_restrict:

surviving_verts.contains(&node.anchor): same hash cost, gains Arc::ptr_eq fast path on equality
node.anchor.clone(): Arc::clone (atomic refcount bump) instead of heap allocation



18.2.3 NameSite and SiteRename
File: crates/panproto-gat/src/ident.rs
All 9 naming sites in panproto are unified under the NameSite enum:
pub enum NameSite {
    EdgeLabel, VertexId, VertexKind, EdgeKind,
    Nsid, ConstraintSort, InstanceAnchor,
    TheoryName, SortName,
}
SiteRename { site, old, new } is a site-qualified rename operation. These are stored in SchemaMorphism provenance and CommitObject rename metadata.


18.2.4 TheoryMorphism.induce_schema_renames()
File: crates/panproto-gat/src/morphism.rs
A theory morphism \(F: T_1 \to T_2\) with sort_map and op_map induces schema-level renames:

Each sort_map entry where old != new becomes a NameSite::VertexKind rename
Each op_map entry where old != new becomes a NameSite::EdgeKind rename

This is analogous to GATlab’s SimpleTheoryMap, which maps type and term constructors between theories while preserving structural equivalence.


18.2.5 SchemaMorphism
File: crates/panproto-schema/src/morphism.rs
An explicit schema morphism (a map \(F: S \to T\)):
pub struct SchemaMorphism {
    pub name: String,
    pub src_protocol: String,
    pub tgt_protocol: String,
    pub vertex_map: HashMap<Name, Name>,
    pub edge_map: HashMap<Edge, Edge>,
    pub renames: Vec<SiteRename>,  // provenance
}
Composition via m1.compose(&m2) chains vertex/edge maps and concatenates renames. This makes the structure of schema transformations explicit, enabling transitive composition of cross-protocol translations.


18.2.6 The Cascade Module
File: crates/panproto-mig/src/cascade.rs
The morphism tower connects three levels:
Theory morphism  ──induce_schema_morphism──→  Schema morphism
                                                    │
                                          induce_data_migration
                                                    │
                                                    ▼
                                            CompiledMigration

induce_schema_morphism(theory_morph, src_schema): Applies sort/op renames to produce a SchemaMorphism
induce_data_migration(schema_morph, tgt_schema): Lowers to CompiledMigration for the restrict pipeline
induce_migration_from_theory(theory_morph, src, tgt): Convenience function chaining both

This implements the action described in Spivak (2012): a schema morphism \(F\) induces the pullback functor \(\Delta_F\) on instances.






TipHow does a theory rename become a data migration?



A sort rename Vertex → Node in the theory becomes a vertex-kind rename in the schema, which becomes an anchor remap in the instance. Each level of the tower translates the same conceptual change into a more concrete representation.




18.2.7 New Combinators
File: crates/panproto-lens/src/combinators.rs
Seven new combinator variants complement the original six:









Combinator
Site
Lossless?
Notes




RenameVertex
VertexId
Yes
Cascades to edges, constraints, variants


RenameKind
VertexKind
Yes
Fine-grained (single vertex)


RenameEdgeKind
EdgeKind
Yes
All edges with matching kind


RenameNsid
Nsid
Yes
Single vertex


RenameConstraintSort
ConstraintSort
Yes
All constraints


ApplyTheoryMorphism
Multiple
Yes
Top of the morphism tower


Rename
Any
Yes
Unified dispatcher



Each combinator has a corresponding apply_* function and build_compiled_migration arm. All are lossless (empty complement), so the lens laws hold trivially.


18.2.8 VCS Rename Detection
File: crates/panproto-vcs/src/rename_detect.rs

18.2.8.1 Algorithm
When comparing old and new schemas:

Collect removed vertices (in old, not in new) and added vertices (in new, not in old)
For each (removed, added) pair, compute structural similarity score:

Same vertex kind: +0.3
Same outgoing edge names: +0.3
Same incoming edge names: +0.2
Edit distance of names <= 3: +0.2

Greedy bipartite matching: take highest-scoring pairs first
Return pairs >= threshold (default 0.5)



18.2.8.2 Integration
Detected renames are stored in CommitObject.renames as Vec<SiteRename>. The schema diff --detect-renames CLI command exposes rename detection.



18.2.9 Migration Path: String to Name
The core types (Vertex, Edge, Schema, Node, CompiledMigration, Migration) use Name for all identifier and label fields. Constraint.value and Schema.protocol remain String since they are literal data, not identifiers.
The SchemaBuilder API still accepts &str parameters, converting to Name internally. Protocol parsers required no changes.
Performance: Benchmarks show no regression from the String to Name migration. The Arc::clone fast path eliminates heap allocations in the restrict pipeline’s inner loop.

title: 18  Advanced Internals: Building Blocks, Naming, Auto-Morphisms, and Type Checking
section: 18.3 Automatic Morphisms: Extend, Restrict, and Complete
text:
18.3 Automatic Morphisms: Extend, Restrict, and Complete
This section covers the automatic homomorphism search, the extend/restrict/complete operations,2 schema-level pushouts, overlap discovery, and the chase algorithm.

18.3.1 Homomorphism Search
File: crates/panproto-mig/src/hom_search.rs

18.3.1.1 CSP Reduction
The homomorphism problem reduces to a constraint satisfaction problem:

Variables: one per source vertex
Domains: target vertices with compatible kind
Constraints: edge-preserving squares



18.3.1.2 Algorithm
Backtracking search with forward checking:

Variable ordering: MRV heuristic (assign vertices with smallest domains first)
Value selection: try each domain value
Forward check: after assignment, verify all unassigned neighbors still have valid domain values via has_compatible_edge
Edge compatibility: requires matching edge kind between mapped vertex pairs; edge names are allowed to differ (name differences only affect quality scoring)
Edge map derivation: once all vertices are assigned, the edge map is determined by finding compatible edges between each pair of mapped vertices, preferring name-matching edges



18.3.1.3 Search Options



Option
Effect




monic
Injective vertex map (tracks used_targets set)


epic
Surjective vertex map (checked at base case)


iso
Bijective (monic + epic)


max_results
Early termination bound


initial
Pre-assigned mappings (domain restricted to singletons)





18.3.1.4 Quality Scoring
compute_quality produces a score in \([0.0, 1.0]\):

50% weight: average name similarity (\(1 - \text{edit\_distance} / \text{max\_length}\)) across vertex pairs
50% weight: fraction of edges where source and target have the same label name



18.3.1.5 Performance
Schemas have \(O(10\text{-}100)\) vertices. The backtracking search with MRV and forward checking handles this scale easily. Worst case is exponential in the number of vertices with identical kinds, but the edge constraints prune aggressively.



18.3.2 Extend (Left Kan Extension, \(\Sigma_F\))
Files: crates/panproto-inst/src/wtype.rs (wtype_extend), crates/panproto-inst/src/functor.rs (functor_extend)

18.3.2.1 W-Type Extend
wtype_extend maps all source nodes into the target schema:

Remap node anchors via vertex_remap
Remap arc edges via edge_remap
Rebuild tree indices

Unlike wtype_restrict, extend keeps all mappable nodes rather than dropping unmapped ones.


18.3.2.2 Functor Extend
functor_extend (pre-existing) handles the relational case:

Copy tables forward via vertex_remap
Compute coproduct (disjoint union) when multiple sources map to one target
Fill missing columns with Value::Null
Offset FK row indices for concatenated tables




18.3.3 Complete (Right Kan Extension, \(\Pi_F\))
File: crates/panproto-inst/src/pi.rs

18.3.3.1 Functor Complete
functor_pi computes the product over fibers:

Build fiber map: for each target vertex, collect source vertices mapping to it
Single-element fibers: copy table directly
Multi-element fibers: Cartesian product of rows with column union
Size gate: reject products exceeding max_product_size



18.3.3.2 W-Type Complete
wtype_pi currently only handles injective migrations (single-element fibers). Multi-element fiber products for trees require Cartesian products of subtrees, which is deferred to a future release.



18.3.4 Schema Pushout
File: crates/panproto-schema/src/colimit.rs

18.3.4.1 SchemaOverlap
Specifies which elements of two schemas are identified:
pub struct SchemaOverlap {
    pub vertex_pairs: Vec<(Name, Name)>,
    pub edge_pairs: Vec<(Edge, Edge)>,
}


18.3.4.2 schema_pushout Algorithm

Merged vertices: all left vertices + non-overlapping right vertices (overlapping right vertices merge with their left counterpart)
Merged edges: all left edges + right edges with remapped vertex references
Constraint/metadata union for overlapping vertices
Build morphisms: left to pushout (identity for left vertices), right to pushout (identity or remapped for right vertices)
Rebuild adjacency indices



18.3.4.3 Universal Property
For any schema \(C\) with morphisms from both left and right that agree on the overlap, there exists a unique morphism from the pushout to \(C\).



18.3.5 Overlap Discovery
File: crates/panproto-mig/src/overlap.rs
Uses find_best_morphism with monic: true in both directions (left to right and right to left), taking whichever produces more vertex pairs. The vertex map entries from the best morphism define the overlap pairs.


18.3.6 Chase Algorithm
File: crates/panproto-mig/src/chase.rs

18.3.6.1 Embedded Dependencies
pub struct EmbeddedDependency {
    pub pattern_vertex: Name,
    pub pattern_values: HashMap<String, Value>,
    pub consequence_vertex: Name,
    pub consequence_values: HashMap<String, Value>,
}


18.3.6.2 Algorithm

Clone instance
For each iteration (up to max_iterations):

For each dependency, find pattern matches without consequences
Add missing consequence rows
If nothing changed, return fixpoint

If limit reached, return ChaseError::NonTermination



18.3.6.3 Termination
Guaranteed for acyclic dependencies (no dependency’s consequence can trigger itself). For cyclic dependencies, the iteration bound prevents infinite loops. panproto’s equational theories are purely algebraic, so termination is guaranteed for all standard schema theories.



18.3.7 From Morphisms to Protolenses
The homomorphism search, extend/restrict/complete operations, and overlap discovery described here feed directly into the protolens engine (Chapter 19) and the automated lens generation pipeline (Chapter 20).

18.3.7.1 Pipeline Connection
The key integration point is the factorize function in panproto-gat/src/factorize.rs. Given a TheoryMorphism produced by hom_search, factorize decomposes it into elementary TheoryEndofunctor steps. Each step maps to an elementary protolens constructor, producing a ProtolensChain that can be instantiated at any schema satisfying the preconditions.
This extends the morphism tower from three levels to four:

Theory morphism (this section): structure-preserving map between GATs.
Factorization (Section 20.3): decomposition into elementary transforms.
Protolens chain (Chapter 19): schema-parameterized family of lenses.
Concrete lens (Chapter 10): instantiated lens for a specific schema pair.



18.3.7.2 Overlap and Symmetric Lenses
The overlap discovery algorithm (find_best_morphism with monic: true) is reused by the protolens pipeline to construct symmetric lenses. Given two schemas with partial overlap, the pipeline factorizes the morphisms from each schema to the overlap, constructs protolens chains in both directions, and combines them into a SymmetricLens. See Section 20.5 for details.


18.3.7.3 Performance Implications
Because hom_search is the most expensive step (exponential worst case), the automated lens pipeline caches morphism search results. Once a morphism is found, factorization and protolens construction are both linear in the theory size, so the incremental cost of producing a protolens chain from a cached morphism is negligible.



18.3.8 Lift Function Reference



Function
Direction
Instance Type
Module




lift_wtype
restrict (\(\Delta_F\))
W-type
lift.rs


lift_functor
restrict (\(\Delta_F\))
Functor
lift.rs


lift_wtype_sigma
extend (\(\Sigma_F\))
W-type
lift.rs


lift_functor_pi
complete (\(\Pi_F\))
Functor
lift.rs


lift_wtype_pi
complete (\(\Pi_F\))
W-type
lift.rs

title: 18  Advanced Internals: Building Blocks, Naming, Auto-Morphisms, and Type Checking
text:
18.4 The Type-Checking Pipeline
Type-checking in panproto operates at three levels, each building on the one below.

18.4.1 Pipeline Overview






flowchart TD
    T["Raw Term / Equation"] --> TC["typecheck_term / typecheck_equation<br>(panproto-gat)"]
    TC -->|GatError| GD["GatDiagnostics<br>(panproto-vcs gat_validate)"]
    GD -->|type_errors, equation_errors| VS["ValidationStatus<br>(panproto-vcs index)"]
    VS -->|Invalid(reasons)| VF["VcsError::ValidationFailed<br>(panproto-vcs repo)"]
    VF -->|miette diagnostic| CLI["CLI error output<br>(panproto-cli)"]

    style TC fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1a73e8
    style GD fill:#fef3e8,stroke:#e87a1a
    style VS fill:#fef3e8,stroke:#e87a1a
    style VF fill:#fee8e8,stroke:#e81a1a
    style CLI fill:#fee8e8,stroke:#e81a1a




Figure 18.1: The type-checking pipeline flows from raw terms through GAT-level checks, VCS-level diagnostics, and CLI-level formatting.





Level 0 (GAT, panproto-gat): The typecheck module provides the primitive type-checking functions. These operate on Term, Equation, and Theory values and return Result<_, GatError>. This level has no knowledge of schemas, migrations, or the VCS.
Level 1 (VCS, panproto-vcs): The gat_validate module wraps the GAT-level functions and collects their results into GatDiagnostics, a structured report of type errors, equation violations, and migration warnings. The index module stores these diagnostics in the staging area, and the repo module consults them during commit.
Level 2 (CLI, panproto-cli): The CLI commands convert VcsError::ValidationFailed into user-facing miette diagnostics with source context, suggestions, and error codes.


18.4.2 Level 0: GAT Type-Checking
The GAT level provides four public functions that form the foundation of the pipeline:

18.4.2.1 typecheck_term
pub fn typecheck_term(
    term: &Term,
    ctx: &VarContext,
    theory: &Theory,
) -> Result<Arc<str>, GatError>
Given a term, a variable typing context, and a theory, infers the output sort of the term. This is the recursive core: for App { op, args }, it looks up op in the theory, typechecks each argument recursively, checks that each argument’s inferred sort matches the operation’s declared input sort, and returns the output sort.


18.4.2.2 infer_var_sorts
pub fn infer_var_sorts(eq: &Equation, theory: &Theory) -> Result<VarContext, GatError>
Infers variable sorts from an equation by walking both sides and collecting sort constraints from operation application sites. This eliminates the need for explicit sort annotations on equation variables; the sorts are determined by the positions where variables appear.


18.4.2.3 typecheck_equation
pub fn typecheck_equation(eq: &Equation, theory: &Theory) -> Result<(), GatError>
Combines infer_var_sorts and typecheck_term: infers the variable context, typechecks both sides, and verifies they produce the same output sort.


18.4.2.4 typecheck_theory
pub fn typecheck_theory(theory: &Theory) -> Result<(), GatError>
Iterates over all equations in a theory and typechecks each one. Returns the first error encountered. This is the function that the VCS layer calls to validate protocol theories.


18.4.2.5 Error Types
All type-checking errors are variants of GatError:



Table 18.1: GatError type-checking variants.










Variant
Meaning
Example




UnboundVariable(name)
Variable not in context
Using x in a term without declaring its sort


OpNotFound(name)
Operation not in theory
Referencing compose in a theory that has no such op


TermArityMismatch { op, expected, got }
Wrong argument count
Passing 1 arg to a binary op


ArgTypeMismatch { op, arg_index, expected, got }
Wrong argument sort
Passing an Edge where Vertex is expected


EquationSortMismatch { equation, lhs_sort, rhs_sort }
Equation sides differ
LHS has sort Edge, RHS has sort Vertex


ConflictingVarSort { var, sort1, sort2 }
Variable at incompatible positions
x used as Vertex on LHS and Edge on RHS






These error types carry enough structured data for downstream layers to produce precise, actionable diagnostics.



18.4.3 Level 1: VCS Integration
The VCS layer integrates type-checking at three points in the schema evolution workflow: staging (schema add), committing (schema commit), and theory validation.

18.4.3.1 GatDiagnostics
The GatDiagnostics struct in panproto-vcs::gat_validate bridges GAT-level errors and VCS-level validation:
pub struct GatDiagnostics {
    pub type_errors: Vec<String>,
    pub equation_errors: Vec<String>,
    pub migration_warnings: Vec<String>,
}

type_errors collects results from typecheck_theory. Each entry is the Display output of a GatError.
equation_errors collects results from check_model_with_options. Each entry describes a specific equation violation with its variable assignment and the values that disagreed.
migration_warnings collects structural warnings from validate_migration; these are non-blocking issues like empty vertex maps or incoherent edge endpoints.

The is_clean() method returns true only when both type_errors and equation_errors are empty. Migration warnings don’t block commits.


18.4.3.2 Integration Point: schema add (Repository::add)
When Repository::add stages a schema, it runs GAT validation on the auto-derived migration:

Compute the schema diff between HEAD and the new schema.
Call auto_mig::derive_migration to produce a Migration.
Call gat_validate::validate_migration to check migration structure; the result populates GatDiagnostics.
Store the diagnostics in the StagedSchema::gat_diagnostics field.
If gat_diagnostics.has_errors(), set ValidationStatus::Invalid(diag.all_errors()).

The index file (.panproto/index.json) persists the diagnostics so that schema commit can consult them without re-running validation.


18.4.3.3 Integration Point: schema commit (Repository::commit_with_options)
When Repository::commit_with_options creates a commit, it checks two gates:

ValidationStatus: if ValidationStatus::Invalid, the commit is blocked with VcsError::ValidationFailed.
GatDiagnostics: even if ValidationStatus is Valid, the commit checks gat_diagnostics.has_errors() as a secondary gate. This handles the case where validation status was manually set to Valid but diagnostics were populated later.

Both gates are bypassed when CommitOptions::skip_verify is true. The --skip-verify CLI flag sets this option, providing an escape hatch for work-in-progress schemas.
pub struct CommitOptions {
    pub skip_verify: bool,
}


18.4.3.4 Integration Point: Theory Validation
The validate_theory_equations function provides standalone theory type-checking:
pub fn validate_theory_equations(theory: &Theory) -> GatDiagnostics
This is called by the schema validate and schema typecheck CLI commands for schemas that aren’t part of a VCS repository. It runs typecheck_theory and collects any errors into GatDiagnostics::type_errors.
The validate_schema_equations function goes further by building a model from the schema and running check_model_with_options:
pub fn validate_schema_equations(
    schema: &Schema,
    theory: &Theory,
    model: &Model,
) -> GatDiagnostics
This checks whether the schema’s interpretation of the theory satisfies all equations. The check uses CheckModelOptions::max_assignments = 10_000 to bound enumeration time. If the assignment count exceeds the limit, the error is reported as a non-blocking diagnostic rather than a hard failure.



18.4.4 Level 2: CLI Error Formatting
The CLI layer converts VcsError::ValidationFailed into user-facing diagnostics using the miette crate.

18.4.4.1 Error Flow
GatError::ArgTypeMismatch { op: "src", arg_index: 0, expected: "Edge", got: "Vertex" }
  -> GatDiagnostics { type_errors: ["type mismatch for src argument 0: expected Edge, got Vertex"] }
    -> ValidationStatus::Invalid(["type error: type mismatch for src argument 0: ..."])
      -> VcsError::ValidationFailed { reasons: ["type error: ..."] }
        -> miette::Report with labeled span and suggestion
Each layer adds context:

GatError: the raw error with structured fields (op, arg_index, expected, got).
GatDiagnostics: the error serialized to a string via Display, prefixed with its category ("type error: " or "equation violation: ").
ValidationStatus: the collected error strings from all_errors().
VcsError: the error wrapped in a ValidationFailed variant with a reasons field.
miette: the error rendered with ANSI colors, source labels, and help text.



18.4.4.2 Equation Violation Formatting
Equation violations receive special formatting in gat_validate::format_violation:
equation 'assoc' violated when a=2, b=3, c=1: LHS=6, RHS=4
This format shows the equation name, the variable assignment that triggered the violation, and the concrete values that the two sides evaluated to. For models with symbolic term representations (as produced by free_model), the values are printed as term strings:
equation 'left_id' violated when a=mul(unit(), unit()): LHS=mul(unit(), mul(unit(), unit())), RHS=mul(unit(), unit())



18.4.5 Cross-Cutting Integration Points
The type-checking pipeline touches several subsystems beyond the primary add/commit path:

18.4.5.1 auto_mig
The auto_mig::derive_migration function in panproto-vcs produces the migration that is subsequently validated. While auto_mig itself doesn’t call type-checking functions, the quality of its output directly affects whether GAT validation passes. A poorly-derived migration (e.g., one that maps vertices to vertices of incompatible kinds) will produce GatDiagnostics with migration warnings.


18.4.5.2 Index Persistence
The StagedSchema struct in panproto-vcs::index stores gat_diagnostics: Option<GatDiagnostics>. This field is serialized to .panproto/index.json via serde. The #[serde(default)] attribute ensures backward compatibility: index files written before the GAT completeness feature was added deserialize with gat_diagnostics: None, which is treated as “no diagnostics available” rather than “diagnostics clean”.


18.4.5.3 Merge and Rebase
The merge and rebase pipelines don’t currently run GAT type-checking on the merged result. Three-way merge operates at the schema level (vertices, edges, constraints), and the merged schema is validated structurally. GAT validation is deferred to the next schema add that stages the merged schema for further evolution.
The merge pipeline does use GAT-level operations: it calls pullback to compute structural overlap between branches, and it constructs migration morphisms that are validated by check_morphism. These are correctness checks on the merge algorithm itself, not on the user’s schema.


18.4.5.4 CLI Commands
The following CLI commands invoke parts of the type-checking pipeline:



Table 18.2: CLI commands and their type-checking behavior.









Command
Type-checking performed




schema validate
typecheck_theory on protocol theory


schema typecheck
typecheck_theory on schema theory; optionally validates migration morphism


schema verify
check_model_with_options on schema model


schema check --typecheck
validate_migration + validate_theory_equations


schema add
validate_migration on auto-derived migration


schema commit
Checks stored GatDiagnostics from staging


schema commit --skip-verify
Bypasses GatDiagnostics check









18.4.6 Design Rationale

18.4.6.1 Why Three Levels
The three-level design separates concerns cleanly:

Level 0 is pure mathematics. It knows about terms, sorts, and operations, nothing about files, schemas, or version control. This makes it easy to test in isolation and reuse in non-VCS contexts (e.g., the WASM boundary, the TypeScript SDK).
Level 1 is integration logic. It knows how to translate between GAT errors and VCS concepts (staging, commits, validation status). It serializes diagnostics for persistence.
Level 2 is presentation. It knows how to render errors for humans with colors, source labels, and suggestions. It uses miette, which is a CLI-only dependency.



18.4.6.2 Why Store Diagnostics in the Index
Storing GatDiagnostics in the index rather than re-computing them at commit time has two benefits:

Performance: type-checking and model checking can be expensive for large theories. Running them once at staging time and persisting the results avoids redundant work.
Auditability: the index file records exactly what diagnostics were produced, even if the user later modifies the schema file. The commit gate checks the stored diagnostics, not the current file state.



18.4.6.3 Why skip_verify Exists
The --skip-verify flag is an explicit escape hatch for situations where the type-checking pipeline produces false positives or where a work-in-progress schema intentionally violates equations. Without this flag, users would be blocked from committing legitimate intermediate states. The flag is named to make its risk visible: you’re skipping verification, not disabling a cosmetic check.


18.4.6.4 Why Equation Checking is Bounded
The max_assignments bound in CheckModelOptions prevents combinatorial explosion. An equation with 3 variables over a carrier of 100 elements requires \(10^6\) assignments; with 4 variables it’s \(10^8\). The default bound of 10,000 catches most violations while keeping verification fast. Users who need exhaustive checking can raise the bound via schema verify --max-assignments.






Cartmell, John. 1986. “Generalised Algebraic Theories and Contextual Categories.” Annals of Pure and Applied Logic 32: 209–43. https://doi.org/10.1016/0168-0072(86)90053-9.


Lynch, Owen, Kris Brown, James Fairbanks, and Evan Patterson. 2024. “GATlab: Modeling and Programming with Generalized Algebraic Theories.” arXiv Preprint arXiv:2404.04837, ahead of print. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2404.04837.


Spivak, David I. 2012. “Functorial Data Migration.” Information and Computation 217: 31–51. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ic.2012.05.001.

title: 18  Advanced Internals: Building Blocks, Naming, Auto-Morphisms, and Type Checking
text:
Rather than expressing injectivity as a conditional equation (which GATs can’t express), panproto adds a retraction operation that witnesses the property structurally. This is Cartmell’s property-to-structure move (Cartmell 1986).↩︎
Extend pushes data forward along a migration (\(\Sigma_F\)), restrict pulls data backward (\(\Delta_F\)), and complete fills in missing data by computing products over fibers (\(\Pi_F\)). These three operations form an adjoint triple \(\Sigma_F \dashv \Delta_F \dashv \Pi_F\) in categorical database theory (Spivak 2012).↩︎
crumbs: 18  Advanced Internals: Building Blocks, Naming, Auto-Morphisms, and Type Checking

title: 19  Protolens Engine
text:
19.1 TheoryEndofunctor and TheoryTransform
A Protolens is a reusable schema transform that produces a concrete lens for any schema matching a precondition. Where a Lens is bound to two specific schemas, a Protolens is a dependent function: given a schema \(S\) and a protocol, it yields a \(\text{Lens}(F(S), G(S))\) where \(F\) and \(G\) are schema transforms.1
For the concrete lens primitives (get, put, complement), see Chapter 10. For the automated pipeline that derives protolens chains from schema diffs, see Chapter 20.
File: crates/panproto-gat/src/schema_functor.rs
A TheoryEndofunctor pairs a precondition with a schema transform, a description of how to modify a theory:
The TheoryTransform enum defines how a schema transform acts on a theory:
Each variant corresponds to an elementary operation on theories:

title: 19  Protolens Engine
19  Protolens Engine
19  Protolens Engine
19  Protolens Engine
19  Protolens Engine
text:
19.5 Commutativity Checking (Order Independence)
A protolens is well-behaved if instantiating it at one schema and then migrating along a schema morphism produces the same result as migrating first and then instantiating. In other words, the order doesn’t matter: the two paths through the following diagram arrive at the same lens.2
For the elementary protolens constructors in protolens::elementary, this commutativity holds by construction: each constructor’s source and target transforms act locally (on a single sort or operation), so their action commutes with schema morphisms that don’t touch the affected elements. Commutativity for composed protolenses follows from commutativity of the components.
In practice, commutativity is verified by the test suite rather than checked at runtime. The ProtolensChain::instantiate method trusts that the chain was constructed from verified components.

title: 19  Protolens Engine
19  Protolens Engine
text:
19.7 Elementary Protolens Constructors
The protolens::elementary module provides nine atomic protolens constructors, each corresponding to one TheoryTransform variant:



Table 19.2: Elementary protolens constructors.










Constructor
Type signature
Lossless?




add_sort(name, kind, default)
\(\text{Id} \Rightarrow \text{AddSort}(\tau)\)
Yes


drop_sort(name)
\(\text{Id} \Rightarrow \text{DropSort}(\tau)\)
No


rename_sort(old, new)
\(\text{Id} \Rightarrow \text{RenameSort}(\text{old}, \text{new})\)
Yes


add_op(name, src, tgt, kind)
\(\text{Id} \Rightarrow \text{AddOp}(\text{op})\)
Yes


drop_op(name)
\(\text{Id} \Rightarrow \text{DropOp}(\text{op})\)
No


rename_op(old, new)
\(\text{Id} \Rightarrow \text{RenameOp}(\text{old}, \text{new})\)
Yes


add_equation(eq)
\(\text{Id} \Rightarrow \text{AddEquation}(\text{eq})\)
Yes


drop_equation(name)
\(\text{Id} \Rightarrow \text{DropEquation}(\text{eq})\)
Yes


pullback(morphism)
\(\text{Id} \Rightarrow \text{Pullback}(\phi)\)
Yes






All elementary constructors have the identity as their source transform. The precondition on the source is Unconstrained for additive constructors (adding a sort/op/equation is always valid) and HasSort/HasOp/HasEquation for subtractive or renaming constructors (the element must exist to be dropped or renamed).
For example, elementary::drop_sort constructs:
                name: Arc::from("id"),
                precondition: TheoryConstraint::Unconstrained,
                transform: TheoryTransform::Identity,
            },
            target: TheoryEndofunctor {
                name: Arc::from(&*format!("add_{sort_name}")),
                precondition: TheoryConstraint::Unconstrained,
                // Use sort_name as the theory sort name — this maps to
                // the vertex ID in apply_theory_transform_to_schema.
                transform: TheoryTransform::AddSort(Sort::simple(name_arc_clone(&sort_name))),
            },
            complement_constructor: ComplementConstructor::AddedElement {
                element_name: sort_name,
                element_kind: format!("{vertex_kind}"),
                default_value: Some(default),
            },
        }
    }

    /// `η : Id ⟹ DropSort(τ)` — for each `S` containing sort `τ`,
    /// `η_S` is a lens `S → S \ {τ}`.

title: 19  Protolens Engine
19  Protolens Engine
text:
19.9 Integration with the Cascade Module
File: crates/panproto-mig/src/cascade.rs
The protolens engine connects to panproto-mig’s morphism tower via the cascade module. The tower has three levels:

Theory morphism: maps sorts to sorts, operations to operations.
Schema morphism (induced by the theory morphism): renames vertex kinds and edge kinds.
Data migration (induced by the schema morphism): the CompiledMigration that transforms instances.

The induce_schema_morphism function in cascade.rs bridges levels 1 and 2. The protolens engine adds a parallel path: instead of requiring a single theory morphism, it factors the morphism into elementary transforms via factorize (Section 20.3) and maps each factor to a protolens. This produces a ProtolensChain that, when instantiated, yields a composed Lens.
The advantage over the direct cascade path is compositionality: each protolens step is independently verifiable, and the chain can be inspected, serialized, and modified without recomputing the full migration.

title: 19  Protolens Engine
text:
19.10 Schema Constraints vs Theory Constraints
TheoryConstraint operates on implicit theories extracted from schemas via schema_to_implicit_theory. This extraction is lossy: vertex IDs are discarded (only kinds survive as sorts), edge names are lost (only kinds survive as operations), and structural properties like edge multiplicities and ordering constraints have no representation in the implicit theory.
SchemaConstraint checks schemas directly, bypassing the lossy extraction. It supports predicates that TheoryConstraint can’t express:

HasVertex(id): the schema must contain a vertex with this ID, not just this kind.
HasEdgeBetween(src, tgt, kind): a specific edge must exist between two specific vertices.
VertexCount(kind, min, max): the schema must have between min and max vertices of the given kind.

The check() method on SchemaConstraint returns a Vec<ConstraintFailure> instead of a boolean. Each failure includes the constraint that failed, the schema element it checked, and a human-readable explanation.
SchemaConstraint::from_theory_constraint bridges the two systems: it converts a TheoryConstraint into the closest SchemaConstraint equivalent by mapping HasSort(n) to “any vertex with kind n exists” and HasOp(n) to “any edge with kind n exists”. The conversion is conservative; it never accepts a schema that the original TheoryConstraint would reject.
Protolens::check_applicability uses SchemaConstraint internally and returns the full list of ConstraintFailure values, giving callers actionable diagnostics instead of a bare boolean.

title: 19  Protolens Engine
text:
19.11 Endofunctor Fusion
The fuse() method on ProtolensChain composes all steps’ target transforms into a single TheoryTransform::Compose tree. Given a chain with steps \([F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n]\), fusion produces a single Protolens whose target transform is \(\text{Compose}(F_1, \text{Compose}(F_2, \ldots \text{Compose}(F_{n-1}, F_n)))\).
The fused protolens requires a single instantiate call, which applies the composed transform to the input schema once. Without fusion, ProtolensChain::instantiate applies each step sequentially, materializing \(n-1\) intermediate schemas. For a chain of length \(n\) with schemas of size \(|S|\), fusion reduces instantiation from \(O(n \cdot |S|^2)\) to \(O(|S|^2)\) (one migration derivation instead of \(n\)).
Complement constructors from all steps merge into a Composite variant, preserving the per-step complement structure for accurate round-trip data capture.
ProtolensChain::instantiate calls fuse() automatically. Calling fuse() explicitly is useful for serializing the composed protolens or inspecting the transform tree.

title: 19  Protolens Engine
text:
19.12 Functorial Lifting
lift_protolens transforms a protolens defined over one protocol’s theories into an equivalent protolens over another protocol’s theories, given a theory morphism between the two protocols.
The implementation has three components:

lift_endofunctor: Given a TheoryEndofunctor \(F\) and a morphism \(\phi : T_1 \to T_2\), produce a new endofunctor \(\phi^* F\) that acts on \(T_2\)-theories. The target transform is composed with Pullback($\phi$): first pull back the schema along the morphism, then apply the original transform, then push forward. The precondition is lifted by renaming sort and operation references through \(\phi\)’s maps.
lift_constraint: Rename sort and operation references in a TheoryConstraint (or SchemaConstraint) via the morphism’s sort map and op map. HasSort("Vertex") becomes HasSort($\phi$.sort_map["Vertex"]).
lift_protolens: Lift both the source and target endofunctors, preserving the complement constructor. A DroppedSortData { sort: "Vertex" } becomes DroppedSortData { sort: $\phi$.sort_map["Vertex"] }.

lift_chain applies lift_protolens to each step in a ProtolensChain.
The categorical semantics: lifting is precomposition with a morphism. A protolens \(\eta : F \Rightarrow G\) over theory \(T_1\) lifts to \(\phi^* \eta : \phi^* F \Rightarrow \phi^* G\) over theory \(T_2\), where \(\phi^*\) denotes pullback along \(\phi\). The naturality of \(\eta\) is preserved by the functoriality of \(\phi^*\).

title: 19  Protolens Engine
text:
19.13 Performance



Table 19.3: Protolens engine performance.










Operation
Complexity
Notes




TheoryConstraint::satisfied_by
\(O(\lvert C\rvert \cdot \lvert T\rvert)\)
\(C\) = constraint tree, \(T\) = theory size


TheoryTransform::apply
\(O(\lvert T\rvert)\)
Linear scan of sorts/ops/eqs


Protolens::instantiate
\(O(\lvert S\rvert^2)\)
Schema comparison for migration derivation


ProtolensChain::instantiate
\(O(n \cdot \lvert S\rvert^2)\)
\(n\) = chain length


factorize (morphism decomposition)
\(O(\lvert\text{sorts}\rvert + \lvert\text{ops}\rvert)\)
Linear in domain/codomain sizes


Vertical composition
\(O(1)\)
Structural only, no computation


Horizontal composition
\(O(1)\)
Structural only, no computation






For typical schemas (10 to 100 vertices), instantiation completes in microseconds. The dominant cost is the migration derivation in compute_migration_between, which performs a nested scan over unmapped vertices for kind-based matching. For schemas with many vertices of the same kind, this quadratic term can become significant, but panproto schemas rarely exceed a few hundred vertices.
Composition (both vertical and horizontal) is \(O(1)\) because it only wraps the operands in new structs; no computation occurs until instantiation. This makes it cheap to build complex protolens chains speculatively and discard them if the preconditions aren’t met.






Cartmell, John. 1986. “Generalised Algebraic Theories and Contextual Categories.” Annals of Pure and Applied Logic 32: 209–43. https://doi.org/10.1016/0168-0072(86)90053-9.


Johnson, Michael, and Robert Rosebrugh. 2013. “Delta Lenses and Opfibrations.” Proceedings of the 2nd International Workshop on Bidirectional Transformations (BX 2013), Electronic communications of the EASST, vol. 57. https://doi.org/10.14279/tuj.eceasst.57.875.


Litt, Geoffrey, Martin Kleppmann, and Marc Shapiro. 2022. Project Cambria: Schema Evolution for CRDTs. Ink & Switch research essay. https://www.inkandswitch.com/cambria/.

title: 19  Protolens Engine
text:
A protolens is a reusable lens template parameterized by a schema. Given any schema meeting a precondition, it produces a concrete lens with concrete get/put operations. The formal type, \(\Pi(S : \mathsf{Schema} \mid P(S)). \mathsf{Lens}(F(S), G(S))\), captures this: \(S\) ranges over schemas satisfying predicate \(P\), and \(F\), \(G\) are schema transforms that produce the source and target of the resulting lens. In the categorical literature, this is a natural transformation between theory endofunctors (Cartmell 1986).↩︎
Order independence means: instantiate-then-migrate produces the same result as migrate-then-instantiate. In the categorical literature, this is the naturality condition for natural transformations. Given a schema morphism \(\phi : S_1 \to S_2\), the equation \(\eta_{S_2} \circ F(\phi) = G(\phi) \circ \eta_{S_1}\) must hold.↩︎
Chaining two protolenses: the first transforms \(F\) to \(G\), the second transforms \(G\) to \(H\), producing a single protolens from \(F\) to \(H\). In categorical notation, this is the vertical composite \(\theta \circ \eta : F \Rightarrow H\).↩︎
Two independent transforms are applied in parallel: one changes sorts/ops in one region of the theory, the other changes a different region. In categorical notation, this is the whiskering product \(\eta * \theta : F \circ F' \Rightarrow G \circ G'\).↩︎

title: 20  Automated Lens Generation Pipeline
text:
20.1 End-to-End Pipeline
The automated lens generation pipeline derives protolens chains from schema diffs. Given two schemas, panproto can search for a homomorphism, factorize it into elementary steps, convert each step to a protolens, and instantiate the resulting chain, all without manual lens construction.
For the protolens engine internals, see Chapter 19. For the homomorphism search and the extend/restrict/complete operations, see Section 18.3.
The automated lens generation pipeline consists of five stages:
flowchart LR
    S1[Schema v1] --> HOM["hom_search"]
    S2[Schema v2] --> HOM
    HOM --> MOR["TheoryMorphism"]
    MOR --> FAC["factorize"]
    FAC --> CHAIN["ProtolensChain"]
    CHAIN --> INST["instantiate"]
    INST --> LENS["Lens"]

    style LENS fill:#e8f4e8,stroke:#2d7d2d




Figure 20.1: End-to-end automated lens generation pipeline. Each stage transforms the representation one step closer to an executable lens.

title: 20  Automated Lens Generation Pipeline
text:
Homomorphism search (hom_search): find a structure-preserving map between the source and target schemas. This uses the CSP-based backtracking search described in Section 18.3.
Factorization (factorize): decompose the resulting theory morphism into a sequence of elementary transforms (drops, renames, and adds).
Protolens chain construction (diff_to_protolens): map each elementary transform to an elementary protolens constructor.
Instantiation: apply the protolens chain to the source schema, producing a concrete Lens.
Application: use the lens’s get and put to migrate data bidirectionally.

title: 20  Automated Lens Generation Pipeline
20  Automated Lens Generation Pipeline
20  Automated Lens Generation Pipeline
text:
20.4 diff_to_protolens: Mapping Transforms to Protolenses
Each TheoryEndofunctor in a factorization maps directly to an elementary protolens constructor:



Table 20.1: Mapping from theory transforms to elementary protolenses.





TheoryTransform variant
elementary:: constructor
Complement




AddSort(s)
add_sort(s.name, s.name, Null)
Empty


DropSort(n)
drop_sort(n)
DroppedSortData


RenameSort{old,new}
rename_sort(old, new)
Empty


AddOp(op)
add_op(op.name, ...)
Empty


DropOp(n)
drop_op(n)
DroppedOpData


RenameOp{old,new}
rename_op(old, new)
Empty


AddEquation(eq)
add_equation(eq)
Empty


DropEquation(n)
drop_equation(n)
Empty


Pullback(morph)
pullback(morph)
Empty






The conversion is mechanical: each transform step becomes a protolens step with the same semantics. The resulting Vec<Protolens> is wrapped in a ProtolensChain for instantiation.

title: 20  Automated Lens Generation Pipeline
section: 20.5 Symmetric Lens Construction from Overlap Discovery
text:
20.5 Symmetric Lens Construction from Overlap Discovery
When two schemas overlap partially, the pipeline constructs a symmetric lens by:

Discovering overlap: use find_best_morphism (from panproto-mig/src/overlap.rs) with monic: true to find the maximal common substructure.
Building protolens chains for each direction: factorize the morphisms from each schema to the overlap, producing two protolens chains (one forward, one backward).1
Instantiating both chains: produce \(\text{Lens}(S_1, \text{overlap})\) and \(\text{Lens}(S_2, \text{overlap})\).
Composing into a symmetric lens: combine the forward lens from \(S_1\) to the overlap with the backward lens from the overlap to \(S_2\).

The overlap serves as the “common view” that both schemas can project onto. Data not in the overlap is captured in the respective complements.






flowchart LR
    S1["Schema S1"] -->|"project"| O["Overlap"]
    S2["Schema S2"] -->|"project"| O
    O -->|"embed"| S1
    O -->|"embed"| S2

    style O fill:#e8f4e8,stroke:#2d7d2d




Figure 20.2: Symmetric lens construction via overlap discovery. The overlap is the greatest common subschema.

title: 20  Automated Lens Generation Pipeline
text:
20.6 WASM Boundary
Protolens chains cross the WASM boundary via handle-based allocation, consistent with panproto’s handle/slab architecture (see Chapter 13):

create_protolens_chain: allocates a ProtolensChain in the Rust slab, returns a u32 handle to JavaScript.
instantiate_protolens: takes a chain handle and a schema handle, instantiates the chain, and returns a lens handle.
protolens_chain_info: returns metadata (step count, complement types) as a MessagePack-encoded blob.

The ComplementConstructor enum is serialized via serde into MessagePack for transmission across the WASM boundary. The TypeScript SDK deserializes it into a discriminated union type.






Tip



Protolens chains are lightweight on the Rust side (no computation until instantiation), so it’s cheap to create multiple chains speculatively in JavaScript, evaluate their applicability, and instantiate only the best match.

title: 20  Automated Lens Generation Pipeline
text:
20.7 CLI Handlers
The CLI exposes protolens functionality through several subcommands:



Table 20.2: CLI subcommands for protolens operations.









Command
Description




prot convert
Convert data between schema versions using the automated pipeline


prot lens
Build a lens from a combinator chain or protolens specification


prot lens-apply
Apply a lens to an instance (get or put direction)


prot lens-verify
Verify GetPut and PutGet laws on a lens with test data


prot lens-compose
Compose two lenses sequentially


prot lens-diff
Compute the protolens chain between two schemas






The lens-diff command is the primary entry point for the automated pipeline:
prot lens-diff --src schema-v1.json --tgt schema-v2.json --protocol json-schema
This runs the full pipeline (hom_search, factorize, diff_to_protolens) and outputs the protolens chain as a JSON array of steps. Each step includes the transform names, preconditions, and complement constructors, providing a human-readable audit trail of the derived migration.
The lens-verify command property-tests the lens laws:
prot lens-verify --lens lens.json --instance data.json --iterations 1000
This generates random modifications to the instance data and verifies that GetPut and PutGet hold for each modification.

title: 20  Automated Lens Generation Pipeline
20  Automated Lens Generation Pipeline
text:
20.9 Complement State Tracking
chain_complement_spec advances a current_schema through each step’s target transform before evaluating the next step’s complement. Each step’s complement is computed against the schema as it exists after all preceding steps. This means a drop_sort("metadata") followed by add_sort("metadata_v2") correctly reports the drop’s captured data count from the original schema and the add’s default requirement from the intermediate schema.
The AddedElement variant of ComplementConstructor handles add_sort and add_op protolenses. These require default values at instantiation time. AddedElement { name, kind } records what the user must supply, and chain.requirements() aggregates these into a complete list of defaults needed for the chain to instantiate.

title: 20  Automated Lens Generation Pipeline
20  Automated Lens Generation Pipeline
text:
20.11 Testing Strategy
The protolens engine uses three testing approaches:

20.11.1 Unit Tests
Each elementary protolens constructor has unit tests verifying:

Applicability: the protolens is applicable to schemas containing the relevant sorts/ops and not applicable otherwise.
Instantiation correctness: the resulting lens has the expected source and target schemas.
Losslessness classification: is_lossless() returns the correct value.



20.11.2 Commutativity Checks
Commutativity is tested by instantiating the same protolens at multiple related schemas and verifying that the resulting lenses commute with schema morphisms between those schemas. The test fixture generates a family of schemas of increasing complexity and checks commutativity for each pair.


20.11.3 Round-Trip Verification
The check_laws function from panproto-lens/src/laws.rs is applied to instantiated protolenses:

Generate a random instance conforming to the source schema.
Apply get to produce a view and complement.
Apply put with the unmodified view and complement.
Verify that the restored instance equals the original (GetPut).
Apply get to the restored instance.
Verify that the new view and complement equal the originals (PutGet).



20.11.4 Property-Based Tests
The integration test suite (tests/integration/tests/lens_laws.rs) includes property-based tests that:

Generate random schema pairs by applying random sequences of elementary protolens constructors to a base schema.
Factorize the resulting morphism.
Verify that validate_factorization passes.
Instantiate the protolens chain and verify lens laws.

This provides coverage over the full pipeline, catching regressions in factorization ordering, schema transformation, and migration derivation.

title: 20  Automated Lens Generation Pipeline
text: Each direction involves a morphism from a full schema to the overlap subschema. Factorizing these morphisms into elementary transforms and mapping them to protolenses yields two chains whose composition forms the symmetric lens.↩︎

title: 21  Data Versioning Internals
text:
21.1 Object model: DataSetObject, ComplementObject, Protocol
The panproto-vcs object model extends beyond schemas and migrations to include data snapshots, complements, and protocol definitions as first-class versioned objects. For the user-facing workflow, see the tutorial’s data versioning chapter.
File: crates/panproto-vcs/src/objects.rs
Three new variants join the Object enum:

title: 21  Data Versioning Internals
text:
pub enum Object {
    Schema(Schema),
    Migration(CompiledMigration),
    Commit(CommitObject),
    Tag(TagObject),
    DataSet(DataSetObject),
    Complement(ComplementObject),
    Protocol(Protocol),
}

21.1.1 DataSetObject
A DataSetObject is a content-addressed snapshot of instance data bound to a specific schema version:
pub struct DataSetObject {
    pub schema_id: ObjectId,
    pub record_count: usize,
    pub data: Vec<u8>, // MessagePack-encoded instances
}
The data field stores all instances as a single MessagePack array. Each element is a serialized WInstance or FInstance. The schema_id links the snapshot to the schema these instances conform to.
Content addressing means two snapshots with identical data and schema produce the same ObjectId. This provides deduplication: if the same records are staged multiple times against the same schema, only one object is stored.


21.1.2 ComplementObject
A ComplementObject stores the data lost during a forward migration step:
pub struct ComplementObject {
    pub migration_id: ObjectId,
    pub data_id: ObjectId,
    pub complement: Vec<u8>, // MessagePack-encoded complement
}

migration_id: the ObjectId of the Migration object that produced this complement.
data_id: the ObjectId of the DataSetObject the complement was extracted from.
complement: the serialized complement data (field values removed by RemoveVertex, edge data removed by RemoveEdge, etc.).

The complement’s content hash depends on all three fields. Two different migrations applied to the same data produce different complement objects even if the complement bytes happen to be identical, because the migration_id differs.


21.1.3 Protocol variant
The Protocol variant stores a complete panproto_protocols::Protocol value. This is the same struct used throughout the codebase; no wrapper or subset. Serialization uses the serde implementation with MessagePack encoding.

title: 21  Data Versioning Internals
text:
21.2 CommitObject extensions
File: crates/panproto-vcs/src/objects.rs
CommitObject gains three fields:
pub struct CommitObject {
    pub schema_id: ObjectId,
    pub parent_ids: Vec<ObjectId>,
    pub migration_id: Option<ObjectId>,
    pub message: String,
    pub author: String,
    pub timestamp: u64,
    pub renames: Vec<SiteRename>,
    // New fields:
    pub protocol_id: Option<ObjectId>,
    pub data_ids: Vec<ObjectId>,
    pub complement_ids: Vec<ObjectId>,
}

protocol_id: optional reference to a Protocol object. When present, this commit is pinned to a specific protocol version. Validation uses this protocol rather than the ambient protocol.
data_ids: references to DataSetObject values staged with this commit. A commit can have zero or more data snapshots (one per data directory).
complement_ids: references to ComplementObject values generated during data migration. These are stored at commit time so that backward migration can retrieve them.







Note



These fields are not serde(default). Existing serialized commits will fail to deserialize. This is a breaking change. Repositories created before this version must be re-initialized.

title: 21  Data Versioning Internals
section: 21.3 Hash functions: canonical forms for data types

title: 21  Data Versioning Internals
text:
21.4 data_mig.rs: migrate_forward/backward algorithms
File: crates/panproto-vcs/src/data_mig.rs

21.4.1 migrate_forward
migrate_forward takes a DataSetObject, a CompiledMigration, and the store, and returns a new DataSetObject plus a ComplementObject:

Deserialize the MessagePack instances from data.data.
For each instance, apply the migration’s lift_wtype (or lift_functor for functor instances), capturing the complement.
Collect all complements into a single MessagePack array.
Construct the new DataSetObject with the target schema’s ObjectId and the migrated instances.
Construct the ComplementObject with the migration’s ObjectId, the original data’s ObjectId, and the complement bytes.
Store both objects and return their ObjectId values.

If the migration is lossless (complement is empty for every instance), no ComplementObject is created. The function returns None for the complement ID.


21.4.2 migrate_backward
migrate_backward takes a DataSetObject, a ComplementObject, and a CompiledMigration:

Invert the migration via panproto_mig::invert.
Deserialize both the data instances and the complement instances.
For each (instance, complement) pair, apply the inverted migration’s lift_wtype with the complement data restored.
Construct a new DataSetObject bound to the source schema.

Backward migration fails with Error::MissingComplement if the complement object can’t be found in the store. Lossless steps don’t need complements; the inverted migration can reconstruct the original data without external input.


21.4.3 detect_staleness
detect_staleness takes a list of DataSetObject IDs and the current HEAD commit’s schema ID:

For each data set, compare data.schema_id against the HEAD schema ID.
If they differ, find the DAG path from data.schema_id to the HEAD schema.
Return a StalenessReport for each stale data set: the path length, the transform types along the path, and whether any step requires a complement.

The function doesn’t migrate data. It’s a read-only diagnostic.


21.4.4 migrate_through_path
migrate_through_path composes migrate_forward along a DAG path:

Walk the commit path from the data’s schema version to the target commit.
At each step, extract the migration from the commit and call migrate_forward.
Chain the resulting DataSetObject into the next step’s input.
Collect all ComplementObject IDs for storage in the final commit.

For backward segments of a path (when the target is an ancestor), the function uses migrate_backward with the stored complements.






NoteWhat if a complement is missing for a backward step?



The function returns Error::MissingComplement with the migration ID and data set ID. This can happen if someone ran schema gc and the complement was collected. The remedy is to re-migrate forward from an older snapshot that still has its complements.

title: 21  Data Versioning Internals
section: 21.5 Complement lifecycle: creation, storage, retrieval, GC
text:
21.5 Complement lifecycle: creation, storage, retrieval, GC
Complements follow the same lifecycle as other objects in the store:

Creation: migrate_forward produces a ComplementObject and writes it to the store.
Storage: The ComplementObject’s ObjectId is recorded in the CommitObject’s complement_ids field.
Retrieval: migrate_backward looks up the complement by walking the commit’s complement_ids and matching on migration_id and data_id.
GC: gc::mark_sweep marks complements reachable from any commit in the reflog. Unreachable complements are deleted.







Tip



Complements are only generated for lossy migrations. A chain of five rename steps produces zero complement objects. A single RemoveVertex step produces one complement object per data set. Storage cost is proportional to the data actually lost, not the number of migration steps.


The retrieval algorithm in migrate_backward is:

Find the commit whose data_ids contains the current data set’s ObjectId.
Scan that commit’s complement_ids for a ComplementObject whose migration_id matches the step’s migration and whose data_id matches the input data set.
If found, deserialize and use. If not found and the step is lossy, return Error::MissingComplement.

title: 21  Data Versioning Internals
section: 21.6 DAG path migration: multi-step data migration through commit history
text:
21.6 DAG path migration: multi-step data migration through commit history
migrate_through_path handles three cases:
Linear path (ancestor to descendant): walk forward through each commit, applying migrate_forward at each step. Each step’s output DataSetObject becomes the next step’s input.
